title: Author Correction: Evaluation of deep learning in non-coding RNA classification

data_scope.primary_dataset: Rfam database (ncRNA family sequences; exact release and subset counts in original article 10.1038/s42256-019-0051-2)
data_scope.sequence_types: miRNA, lncRNA, rRNA, tRNA, snRNA, snoRNA and other ncRNA families
data_scope.note: Specific dataset volumes and model configurations are documented in the corrected original article, not reproduced in this correction notice

core_conclusion: This Author Correction amends the original research article (10.1038/s42256-019-0051-2). The corrected study demonstrated that deep learning architectures, particularly CNNs, significantly outperform classical machine learning methods for ncRNA family classification, while also showing that imbalanced and homology-redundant training sets can inflate reported performance metrics, calling for stricter evaluation protocols in computational genomics.
why_it_matters: First rigorous benchmark of deep learning vs. classical ML for ncRNA classification; exposes evaluation pitfalls from sequence homology leakage and class imbalance; validates CNNs as end-to-end learners without manual feature engineering; supports biomarker discovery for ncRNA-associated diseases including cancer.
why_nature: Aligns with Nature Machine Intelligence's benchmarking culture for reproducible head-to-head comparisons; methods transfer broadly to any nucleotide or amino-acid sequence classification task; correction notice reflects the journal's commitment to scientific record integrity; occupies a foundational evaluation niche at the ML-life sciences interface.
added_value_to_my_research: Homology-aware train/test split logic inspires spatially-buffered cross-validation to prevent spatial leakage in geospatial ML pipelines
Class imbalance findings (AUC over accuracy for rare classes) directly inform 2SFCA accessibility gap detection where underserved zones are rare labels
Architecture-first methodology guides late-vs-early fusion decisions in multi-source spatiotemporal health models
Benchmark harness design provides a template for structured explainability evaluations comparing SHAP maps across spatial ML architectures
Fine-tuning evaluation protocol (adapting for spatial leakage) is the right design to assess geospatial foundation model transfer
